raw_response:
**Overall rating: MISLEADING.** GitHub does link commits to accounts by commit author email, but the statement overstates how the contributors list works and implies all commits from multiple verified emails always collapse into one contributor entry, which is not how the graph is described in GitHub’s docs.[1][2][3]

## Summary
GitHub’s contributors graph counts commits whose author email is connected to a GitHub account, and GitHub says you can add multiple verified emails to one account.  However, the graph also has limits and exclusions, including only showing the top 100 contributors, excluding merge and empty commits, and requiring commits to be on the default branch to appear.  The “collapse into a single contributor entry” part is plausible in practice for the same user, but GitHub does not describe the graph as simply grouping by raw email across all commits in the way the claim states.[2][3][1]

## Claim 1
**Claim:** “github.com's contributors list is driven by matching the raw author email of each commit to a registered email on a GitHub account.”  
**Rating:** TRUE.  
**Evidence:** GitHub says that if the email address used to author commits is not connected to a GitHub account, those commits won’t be linked to the account and the contributor won’t appear in the graph. GitHub also says commits are attributed when you add the email address to your account, and that GitHub uses the email address in local Git configuration to associate commits pushed from the command line with your account.[1][2]
**Sources:** [https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/viewing-activity-and-data-for-your-repository/viewing-a-projects-contributors](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/viewing-activity-and-data-for-your-repository/viewing-a-projects-contributors), [https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/how-tos/email-preferences/setting-your-commit-email-address](https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/how-tos/email-preferences/setting-your-commit-email-address)

## Claim 2
**Claim:** “If multiple verified emails on one account are used as authors across different commits, all those commits collapse to a single contributor entry on github.com.”  
**Rating:** MISLEADING.  
**Evidence:** GitHub does let one account have multiple verified email addresses, and those emails can be associated with commits and contributions.  But the contributors graph is also limited to the top 100 contributors and depends on commits being on the default branch; GitHub does not state that all commits from all verified emails are guaranteed to collapse into one visible contributor entry in every case.  The claim mixes a generally true attribution rule with an overbroad conclusion about how the visible contributors list is formed.[3][2][1]
**Sources:** [https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/how-tos/email-preferences/adding-an-email-address-to-your-github-account](https://docs.github.com/en/account-and-profile/how-tos/email-preferences/adding-an-email-address-to-your-github-account), [https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/viewing-activity-and-data-for-your-repository/viewing-a-projects-contributors](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/viewing-activity-and-data-for-your-repository/viewing-a-projects-contributors)
